what: Example: Creating a Beta-tree histogram for 2-dim Gaussian data
title: Creating a Beta Tree Histogram
text: illustration, create Beta-tree histogram mixture two-dimensional Gaussian data (n=n = 2000) sampled following distribution visualized : 25N((−1.50.6),(10.50.51))+35N((2−1.5),(1001)), \frac{2}{5} N\left(\left(\begin{matrix}-1.5 \\0.6\end{matrix}\right),\left(\begin{matrix}1 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 1\end{matrix}\right) \right) + \frac{3}{5} N\left(\left(\begin{matrix}2 \\-1.5\end{matrix}\right),\left(\begin{matrix}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{matrix}\right) \right),  use BuildHist() function create Beta-tree histogram. (plot = T, function plots Beta-tree histogram data two-dimensional. )  Besides data matrix X, input two parameters: significance level alpha = 0.1 multiple testing correction method method = "weighted_bonferroni". means confidence intervals histogram provides simultaneously valid every region (.e. bin) confidence level 1-alpha = 0.9. return value BuildHist matrix describing region Beta-tree histogram. row represents one region, histogram includes 26 regions. Let’s look first region histogram: columns provide following information: first two columns lower bounds xx yy-coordinates region; next two columns upper bounds xx yy-coordinates region, .e., rectangle defined bounds [ -2.61 -1.45 ] *[ -0.71 -0.32 ]. data higher dimension, correspondingly columns. fifth column stores empirical density region sixth seventh columns store lower upper confidence bound (CI) average probability density region. probability least (1−α)(1-\alpha), CIs cover average densities regions. eighth column stores number observations region last column stores depth region k-d tree, describe next section. histogram rectangular boundary. explain next sections, histogram includes bounded regions. Alternatively, can initialize bounding box histogram specify bounded = T. need provide two additional parameters option q (explain next section). , initialize bounding box 0.05 0.95 quantiles dimension.  next section, describe Beta-tree histogram constructed.
code: hist <- BuildHist(X, alpha = 0.1, method = "weighted_bonferroni", plot = T) hist[1,] #> [1] -2.61449293 -0.71307863 -1.45076293 -0.31525842  0.03456057  0.01762324 #> [7]  0.05868752 31.00000000  6.00000000 hist <- BuildHist(X, alpha = 0.1, method = "weighted_bonferroni", bounded = T,                    option = "qt", q=c(0.05,0.05),                    plot = TRUE)

what: How a Beta-tree histogram is constructed
title: Creating a Beta Tree Histogram
text: Beta tree histogram constructed four steps: Building k-d tree. Calculating confidence levels every region. Setting confidence bounds. Select regions pass goodness--fit test. can think algorithm taking two passes: top-pass partitioning sample space small regions bottom-pass selecting largest regions observations approximately uniform.

title: Creating a Beta Tree Histogram
text: construction Beta-tree histogram starts iteratively partitioning sample space along marginal sample medians. motivated k-d trees, spatial-partitioning data structure organizing observations k-dimensional space. detail, start observations (root node). step, choose (1) axis along partition (2) location partitioning. (1), iterate coordinates, .e., first partition along xx-axis second partition along yy-axis etc. (2), partition along sample median partitioning coordinate. two-dimensional example , first partition, split sample space two half-spaces: R1={x∈ℝ2:x1<X1,⌈n/2⌉},R2={x∈ℝ2:x1>X1,⌈n/2⌉} R_1 = \{x\\mathbb{R}^2:x_1<X_{1,\lceil{n/2}\rceil} \}, \quad R_2 = \{x\\mathbb{R}^2:x_1>X_{1,\lceil{n/2}\rceil} \}  Note observations sample median included two regions. second step, split R1R_1 R2R_2 respectively. R1R_1 split two children R3={x∈R1:x2<X2,⌈n1/2⌉1},R4={x∈R1:x2>X2,⌈n1/2⌉1}, R_3 = \{x\R_1:x_2<X^1_{2,\lceil{n_1/2}\rceil} \}, \quad R_4 = \{x\R_1:x_2>X^1_{2,\lceil{n_1/2}\rceil} \},  X2,⌈n1/2⌉1X^1_{2,\lceil{n_1/2}\rceil} ⌈n1/2⌉\lceil{n_1/2}\rceil order statistic yy-coordinate observations X1X^1 R1R_1 (total number observations R1R_1 n1n_1). continue splitting region number observations inside less 4logn4\log n, nn total number observations. function BuildKDTree constructs k-d tree. tree object two components: kdtree nd. object kdtree stores root node k-d tree list (can refer function documentation detail). point leftchild rightchild point two partitions region, low ad lower upper bounds region ndat number observations. following one node tree: nd contains number bounded regions depth dd tree (root node depth = 0). useful adjust confidence levels depth account multiple CI. , depth 4 4 bounded regions. can see, bounded regions first 4 levels. can initialize bounding box option bounded = T . restricts histogram construction data inside bounding box. provide two options specify bounding box: option = 'qt', set bounds sample quantiles. example, q = c(0.05, 0.05), construct bounding box R0R_0 follows: vertical boundaries R0R_0 given 0.05 0.95 quantiles data x-coordinate. Denote data fall strictly vertical boundaries X1X^1. horizontal boundaries R0R_0 given 0.05 0.95 quantiles X1X^1 y coordinate. root node R0R_0 interior bounding box. option = 'ndat', specify number observations rather quantiles. example, q = c(25, 25), vertical boundaries R0R_0 x-coordinates equal 25th smallest 25th largest x-coordinates data XX.
code: tree <- BuildKDTree(X, bounded = F) tree$kdtree$leftchild$leftchild$rightchild$rightchild$leftchild$leftchild #> $leftchild #> NULL #>  #> $rightchild #> NULL #>  #> $ndat #> [1] 30 #>  #> $depth #> [1] 6 #>  #> $low #> [1] -1.4507629 -0.7649166 #>  #> $up #> [1] -0.6637458 -0.0331637 #>  #> $lower #> NULL #>  #> $upper #> NULL #>  #> $bounded #> [1] 1 #>  #> $leaf #> [1] TRUE tree$nd #> [1]  0  0  0  0  4 12 36 21

what: Calculating confidence levels for every region
title: Creating a Beta Tree Histogram
text: able construct exact CI probability content every region probability region RkR_k satisfies F(Rk)∼Beta(nk+1,n−nk), F(R_k)\sim \mathrm{Beta}(n_k+1, n-n_k),  nkn_k number observations RkR_k nn total number observations. result, can construct (1−α)(1-\alpha) confidence interval average density f(Rk)=F(Rk)/|Rk|f(R_k) = F(R_k)/|R_k|, |Rk||R_k| denotes volume RkR_k: (qBeta(α2,nk+1,n−nk)|Rk|,qBeta(1−α2,nk+1,n−nk)|Rk|). \left(\frac{\mathrm{qBeta}(\frac{\alpha}{2},n_k+1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}, \frac{\mathrm{qBeta}(1-\frac{\alpha}{2},n_k+1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}\right). want simultaneously cover regions level (1−α)(1-\alpha), can redefine significance level region total probability mis-coverage α\alpha. example, can use weighted Bonferroni adjustment: assign level every bounded region tree depth, significance level regions tree depth DD defined αD\alpha_D, α̂D=αND(Dmax−D+2)∑B=2Dmax−Dmin+21B,D≥Dmin \hat{\alpha}_D = \frac{\alpha}{N_D (D_{\max} - D + 2)   \sum_{B=2}^{D_{\max}-D_{\min}+2}\frac{1}{B}},\quad D\geq D_\min , DmaxD_{\max} maximum depth k-d tree, DminD_{\min} smallest depth (greater 0) bounded regions, NDN_D number bounded regions depth DD, factor ∑B=2Dmax−Dmin+21B\sum_{B=2}^{D_{\max}-D_{\min} + 2}\frac{1}{B} ensures α̂D\hat{\alpha}_D adds α\alpha. function ConfLevel computes α̂D\hat{\alpha}_D depth DD given overall level α\alpha. example, following code computes α̂D\hat{\alpha}_D α=0.1\alpha = 0.1. can also use unweighted Bonferroni adjustment setting method = 'bonferroni'. unweighted Bonferroni adjustment gives equal weight tree depth, equal weight bounded regions tree depth.

title: Creating a Beta Tree Histogram
text: define confidence levels, compute simultaneous confidence intervals average densities f(Rk)f(R_k) (qBeta(α̂D2,nk+1,n−nk)|Rk|,qBeta(1−α̂D2,nk+1,n−nk)|Rk|). \left(\frac{\mathrm{qBeta}(\frac{\hat{\alpha}_D}{2}, n_k + 1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}, \frac{\mathrm{qBeta}(1 - \frac{\hat{\alpha}_D}{2}, n_k + 1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}\right). order construct Beta-tree histogram, use bottom-pass select largest regions confident observations inside uniform distribution. implement idea picking largest RkR_k whose empirical density hk=nk+1n|Rk|h_k = \frac{n_k + 1}{n|R_k|} falls inside CIs children. intersection bounds can computed recursively $$ \mathrm{lower}(R_k) = \max\left(\frac{\mathrm{qBeta}(\frac{\hat{\alpha}_D}{2}, n_k + 1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}, \mathrm{lower}(\text{1st child}), \mathrm{lower}(\text{2nd child})\right)\\ \mathrm{upper}(R_k) = \min\left(\frac{\mathrm{qBeta}(1-\frac{\hat{\alpha}_D}{2}, n_k + 1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}, \mathrm{upper}(\text{1st child}), \mathrm{upper}(\text{2nd child})\right) $$ function SetBounds computes bounds. Let’s compare node k-d tree . Whereas lower upper levels empty , two values now filled .
code: tree <- SetBounds(tree$kdtree, ahat, n) tree$leftchild$leftchild$rightchild$rightchild$leftchild$leftchild #> $leftchild #> NULL #>  #> $rightchild #> NULL #>  #> $ndat #> [1] 30 #>  #> $depth #> [1] 6 #>  #> $low #> [1] -1.4507629 -0.7649166 #>  #> $up #> [1] -0.6637458 -0.0331637 #>  #> $lower #> [1] 0.0135554 #>  #> $upper #> [1] 0.04605867 #>  #> $bounded #> [1] 1 #>  #> $leaf #> [1] TRUE

what: Selecting regions to form the Beta tree histogram
title: Creating a Beta Tree Histogram
text: function SelectNodes selects largest regions (w.r.t inclusion) empirical density hkh_k falls inside interval [lower(Rk),upper(Rk)][\mathrm{lower}(R_k), \mathrm{upper}(R_k)]. row hist matrix represents one region Beta-tree histogram. matrix contains 2×d+52\times d + 5 columns. first 2×d2\times d columns store lower (first dd columns) upper bounds region. (2d+1)(2d+1) column stores empirical density hkh_k next two columns store confidence interval f(Rk)f(R_k) defined previous section: (qBeta(α̂D2,nk+1,n−nk)|Rk|,qBeta(1−α̂D2,nk+1,n−nk)|Rk|). \left(\frac{\mathrm{qBeta}(\frac{\hat{\alpha}_D}{2}, n_k + 1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}, \frac{\mathrm{qBeta}(1 - \frac{\hat{\alpha}_D}{2}, n_k + 1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}\right). last two columns store number observations inside region tree depth. function BuildHist returns hist.
code: B <- matrix(nrow = 0, ncol = (2*d + 5))  hist <- SelectNodes(tree, B, ahat, n)

what: Example: Detecting modes in a mixture of 2-dim Gaussian
title: Mode Hunting Using Beta-Trees
text: example, simulate n=n= 2000 obs. 2-dim Gaussian mixture showed 25N((−1.50.6),(10.50.51))+35N((2−1.5),(1001)), \frac{2}{5} {N}\left(\left(\begin{matrix}-1.5 \\0.6\end{matrix}\right),\left(\begin{matrix}1 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 1\end{matrix}\right) \right) + \frac{3}{5} {N}\left(\left(\begin{matrix}2 \\-1.5\end{matrix}\right),\left(\begin{matrix}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{matrix}\right) \right), start creating Beta-tree histogram. , set confidence level alpha = 0.1 use weighted Bonferroni method multiple testing correction. densities higher upper left lower right regions, suggesting two modes distribution.  use FindModes() function identify modes Beta-tree histogram. function three parameters, first Beta-tree histogram (output BuildHist function), second data dimension d, third cutoff value path length (check paths whose length cutoff). set cutoff=1000 , means look every path two regions. However, might infeasible check every path computational restraint, typically set smaller cutoff, e.g., cutoff = 6. FindModes function returns list three components: mode returns index modes, hist returns input histogram, g returns adjacency graph regions histogram. highlight two modes histogram, asterisks two true modes. two modes Beta-tree histogram close actual modes distribution.  next section, describe details FindModes function.
code: hist <- BuildHist(X, alpha = 0.1, method = "weighted_bonferroni", plot= T) modes <- FindModes(hist = hist, d = d, cutoff = 1000) modes$mode # which regions are the modes? ## [1] 20  8 hist[modes$mode, 1:4] # lower and upper bounds of the two modes ##           [,1]       [,2]      [,3]       [,4] ## [1,]  1.079403 -1.4891923  2.193951 -0.8053697 ## [2,] -1.738694  0.8557582 -1.246862  1.3160574

what: Identifying modes of a Beta-tree histogram
title: Mode Hunting Using Beta-Trees
text: Let’s rephrase question mode detection following: can tell two regions highlighted red (let’s call R1R_1 R2R_2) two distinct modes? (know , can detect modes iterating regions , compare one current list distinct modes, declare mode distinct current modes.) Suppose R1R_1 R2R_2 distinct modes, along every path connects R1R_1 R2R_2, exist least one region RR whose average density lower R1R_1 R2R_2. Beta-tree histogram returns CI average density, compare lower upper bound CI, , exist region RR CIup(R)<min(CIlow(R1),CIlow(R2)), \mathrm{CI}_\mathrm{}(R) < \min(\mathrm{CI}_\mathrm{low}(R_1), \mathrm{CI}_\mathrm{low}(R_2)),  [CIlow(R),CIup(R)][\mathrm{CI}_\mathrm{low}(R), \mathrm{CI}_\mathrm{}(R)] confidence interval average density RR. example, plot confidence intervals every region along shortest path R1R_1 R2R_2. can see, upper confidence bound third region lower endpoints modes. Suppose true every path, declare R1R_1 R2R_2 two distinct modes.  Note examined one path , check every path, use function is_connected(). returns connected two regions distinct modes. function five input values: j indices two regions, g graph based adjacency matrix (describe later), ci lower upper confidence bounds, cutoff specifies maximum length path function checks. need adjacency matrix order compute path two regions. function compute_adjacency_mat() computes adjacency matrix regions histogram. two inputs function Beta-tree histogram hist data dimension d. output adjacency matrix AA, Ai,j=1A_{,j} = 1 ii-th jj-th regions neighbors 0 otherwise. say R1R_1 R2R_2 neighbors [x1,jlow,x1,jup]∩[x2,jlow,x2,jup]≠∅,∀j=1,…,d. [x^{\mathrm{low}}_{1,j}, x^{\mathrm{}}_{1,j}] \cap [x^{\mathrm{low}}_{2,j}, x^{\mathrm{}}_{2,j}] \neq \emptyset,\quad \forall j=1,\ldots, d.   , represent region lower upper bound coordinate, .e., R1R_1 can represented (x1,1low,x1,1up)×…×(x1,dlow,x1,dup)(x^{\mathrm{low}}_{1,1}, x^{\mathrm{}}_{1,1})\times \ldots \times (x^{\mathrm{low}}_{1,d}, x^{\mathrm{}}_{1,d}). adjacency matrix, compute undirected graph g (using function graph_from_adjacency_matrix igraph package). summarize, FindModes proceeds following steps: Order regions empirical density (descending order). Initiate list modes region highest density. Iterate every region order given (1), test distinct mode current list modes using is_connected function. unconnected current modes, add list modes. Return list modes.
code: is_connected(i = modes$mode[1], j = modes$mode[2], g = g, ci = hist[,6:7], cutoff = 6) ## [1] "unconnected" adj <- compute_adjacency_mat(hist = hist, d = d)  adj[1:5, 1:5] ##      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] ## [1,]    0    1    0    0    1 ## [2,]    1    0    0    0    1 ## [3,]    0    0    0    1    1 ## [4,]    0    0    1    0    0 ## [5,]    1    1    1    0    0 g <- igraph::graph_from_adjacency_matrix(adj, mode = "undirected")

Beta-tree histogram provides succinct description data, well simultaneous confidence intervals average density region.

title: Build a Beta-tree histogram — BuildHist
text: Compute Beta-tree histogram multivariate data given confidence level.

title: Build a Beta-tree histogram — BuildHist
code: BuildHist(   X,   alpha = 0.1,   method = "weighted_bonferroni",   bounded = F,   plot = F,   ... )

title: Build a Beta-tree histogram — BuildHist
text: X data matrix size n d. alpha Significance level, default alpha = 0.1. CI covers average density every region Beta-tree histogram simultaneously probability \(1-\alpha\). method Use method = "bonferroni" method = "weighted_bonferroni"(default) adjust multiple inference. bounded bounded = TRUE, create initial bounding box according user input parameters: option Define bounding box order statistics (option = ndat) quantiles (option = qt) observations excluded. qt vector length d equal data dimension. Order statistics quantiles obs. excluded dimension. E.g. 2-dim data, option = "ndat" qt = c(50, 40), boundary x-axis 50-th (n-50 +1)-th order statistics x-axis. boundary y-axis defined 40-th \(n_1 - 40 + 1\) order statistics y-axis obs. inside boundary defined x-axis previous step. Another way put exclude 50 obs. lower upper end x-coordinate, obs. within, exclude 40 obs. lower upper end y-coordinate. option = "qt" qt = c(0.05, 0.1), boundaries defined \(\lceil 0.05 n \rceil\) \(n - \lceil 0.05 n \rceil + 1\) quantiles x-axis, \(\lceil 0.1 n_1 \rceil\) \(n_1 - \lceil 0.1 n_1 \rceil + 1\) quantiles y-axis among observations first boundary defined x-axis. Equivalently, exclude  \(\lceil 0.05 n \rceil\) obs. lower upper end x-coordinate, obs. within, exclude  \(\lceil 0.1 n_1 \rceil\) obs. lower upper end y-coordinate. plot TRUE (default), plot histogram data two-dimensional. ... Additional parameter bounded = T.

title: Build a Beta-tree histogram — BuildHist
text: matrix. row represents one region histogram. first \(d\) columns lower bounds region; next \(d\) columns upper bounds (\(d\) data dimension); \(2d+1\) column stores empirical density region; next two columns  lower upper confidence bounds average density region; last two columns number obs. inside region depth node k-d tree.

title: Build a Beta-tree histogram — BuildHist
text: Construct Beta-tree histogram (1) iteratively partitioning sample space along sample median (similar k-d tree). (2) constructing simultaneous confidence intervals (level \(1-\alpha\)) regions (adjust multiple inference Bonferroni weighted Bonferroni adjustment). (3) selecting largest bounded regions (w.r.t. inclusion) pass goodness fit (GOF) test, .e., empirical density lies confidence intervals average densities rectangles contained region (.e., descendants node k-d tree).

title: Build a Beta-tree histogram — BuildHist
code: X <-  matrix(rnorm(2000, 0, 1), ncol = 2) B <- BuildHist(X) # Plot the histogram B <- BuildHist(X, plot = TRUE)  # Set significance level and multiple hypothesis testing correction B <- BuildHist(X, alpha = 0.05, method = "weighted_bonferroni") # Initialize a bounding box B <- BuildHist(X, alpha = 0.05, method = "weighted_bonferroni",      bounded = TRUE, option = "ndat", qt = c(1,1))

title: Build a k-d tree — BuildKDTree
text: Grow k-d tree iteratively partitioning along sample median coordinate.

title: Build a k-d tree — BuildKDTree
code: BuildKDTree(X, bounded = F, ...)

title: Build a k-d tree — BuildKDTree
text: X data matrix size n d. bounded bounded = TRUE, create initial bounding box according user input parameters: option Define bounding box order statistics (option = ndat) quantiles (option = qt) observations excluded. qt vector length d equal data dimension. Order statistics quantiles obs. excluded dimension. E.g. 2-dim data, option = "ndat" qt = c(50, 40), boundary x-axis 50-th (n-50 +1)-th order statistics x-axis. boundary y-axis defined 40-th \(n_1 - 40 + 1\) order statistics y-axis obs. inside boundary defined x-axis previous step. Another way put exclude 50 obs. lower upper end x-coordinate, obs. within, exclude 40 obs. lower upper end y-coordinate. option = "qt" qt = c(0.05, 0.1), boundaries defined \(\lceil 0.05 n \rceil\) \(n - \lceil 0.05 n \rceil + 1\) quantiles x-axis, \(\lceil 0.1 n_1 \rceil\) \(n_1 - \lceil 0.1 n_1 \rceil + 1\) quantiles y-axis among observations first boundary defined x-axis. Equivalently, exclude  \(\lceil 0.05 n \rceil\) obs. lower upper end x-coordinate, obs. within, exclude  \(\lceil 0.1 n_1 \rceil\) obs. lower upper end y-coordinate. ... Additional parameters bounded = TRUE.

title: Build a k-d tree — BuildKDTree
text: build_tree returns two values: kdtree k-d tree built root node. node contains following information: depth Tree depth region. depth root node 0. ndat number obs. region. low, lower upper bound region, exists. lower, upper lower upper confidence bound average density. bounded whether region bounded. region bounded (low, ) exist. leaf whether node leaf . node leaf number obs. less \(4 \log n\), \(n\) total number obs. Stop partitioning node leaf. leftchild, rightchild pointers left right child node. leftchild points node representing region less median partitioning dimension, rightchild points node representing region greater median. nd number bounded regions every level, starting level 0.

title: Build a k-d tree — BuildKDTree
text: BuildKDTree constructs kd-tree data iteratively partitioning along sample median \(j\)-th coordinate, \(j\) cycles \(1,2,\ldots, d\). Stop partitioning node number obs. inside less \(4\log(n)\).

title: Build a k-d tree — BuildKDTree
code: # build a k-d tree for standard normal samples X <- matrix(rnorm(1000), ncol = 2) kd <- BuildKDTree(X) # Initialize a bounding box kd <-  BuildKDTree(X, bounded = TRUE, option = "ndat", qt = c(1,1))

what: Set significance level for regions at each tree depth — ConfLevel
title: Set significance level for regions at each tree depth — ConfLevel
text: Computes significance level tree depth.

title: Set significance level for regions at each tree depth — ConfLevel
code: ConfLevel(nd, alpha, method)

title: Set significance level for regions at each tree depth — ConfLevel
text: nd vector. Number bounded regions every depth (starting depth 0). alpha Numeric. significance level (average density) regions sums \(\alpha\). method Use method = "bonferroni" method = "weighted_bonferroni" method adjust multiple hypothesis testing.

title: Set significance level for regions at each tree depth — ConfLevel
text: vector significance level \(\alpha_D\) every tree depth (starting depth = 0)

title: Set significance level for regions at each tree depth — ConfLevel
text: root node automatically significance level 0. method = "bonferroni", significance level depth d $$ \hat{\alpha}_d =  \frac{\alpha}{(d_\max - d_\min + 1) * n_d}, d\geq d_\min, $$ \(d_\max\) maximum tree depth, \(d_\min\) minimum depth (greater 0) bounded regions, \(n_d\) number bounded regions depth \(d\). method = weighted_bonferroni, confidence level depth d $$ \hat{\alpha}_d = \frac{\alpha}{(d_\max - d + 2) n_d \sum_{B=2}^{d_\max - d_\min + 2}1/B},d\geq d_\min. $$

what: Identifies distinct modes in Beta-trees histogram — FindModes
title: Identifies distinct modes in Beta-trees histogram — FindModes
text: Compute modes histogram.

title: Identifies distinct modes in Beta-trees histogram — FindModes
code: FindModes(hist, d, cutoff = 6)

title: Identifies distinct modes in Beta-trees histogram — FindModes
text: hist matrix. row represents one region histogram. histogram constructed d dimensional data, first d columns contain lower bounds coordinate, next d columns contain upper bounds. 2d + 1 column contains empirical density. hist can output BuildHist SelectNodes function. d numeric value data dimension. cutoff Maximum path length. default, evaluate paths length cutoff = 6.

title: Identifies distinct modes in Beta-trees histogram — FindModes
text: mode vector candidate modes. hist input Beta-tree histogram. g graph object constructed using adjacency matrix regions histogram.

title: Identifies distinct modes in Beta-trees histogram — FindModes
text: Two regions j distinct modes along every path connecting , exists one region whose upper confidence limit lower confidence limit j. function iterates regions Beta-tree histogram descending order empirical density marks region mode distinct mode currently discovered distinct modes.

title: Identifies distinct modes in Beta-trees histogram — FindModes

what: Plot a 2D Beta tree histogram — PlotHist
title: Plot a 2D Beta tree histogram — PlotHist
text: Draw Beta tree histogram.

title: Plot a 2D Beta tree histogram — PlotHist
code: PlotHist(X, B, nsample = 10000, ...)

title: Plot a 2D Beta tree histogram — PlotHist
text: X data matrix size n 2. B matrix rectangles returned function SelectNodes BuildHist. nsample maximum number data points display plot. ... additional parameter graph: low, mid, high specifies colors scale_fill_gradient2 function.

title: Plot a 2D Beta tree histogram — PlotHist
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot a 2D Beta tree histogram — PlotHist
text: PlotHist plots histogram superimposed original dataset. number data points exceeds nsample, randomly select nsample obs. show graph. Default value \(nsample=10^4\)

title: Plot a 2D Beta tree histogram — PlotHist
code: X <-  matrix(rnorm(2000, 0, 1), ncol = 2) rect <- BuildHist(X, plot = FALSE) PlotHist(X, rect)

what: Select nodes in the histogram — SelectNodes
title: Select nodes in the histogram — SelectNodes
text: Extract maximal (w.r.t. inclusion) nodes bounded whose empirical density within confidence bounds.

title: Select nodes in the histogram — SelectNodes
code: SelectNodes(node, B, ahat, n)

title: Select nodes in the histogram — SelectNodes
text: node k-d tree output SetBounds. B matrix containing nodes selected. ahat vector significance levels depth, starting depth = 0. Output funtion ConfLevel n Total number observations.

title: Select nodes in the histogram — SelectNodes
text: matrix selected regions within including input node. row represents one selected region \(2d + 5\) columns, containing lower upper bounds region, empirical density, lower upper confidence bounds average density region, number obs. inside depth node.

title: Select nodes in the histogram — SelectNodes
text: bounded node selected empirical density inside confidence bounds children. empirical density defined $$ h = \frac{n+1}{N |R|}, $$ \(N\) total number obs., \(n\) number obs. region, \(|R|\) volume region. node selected leaf, move left right children node.

what: Add confidence bounds to bounded regions in the k-d tree — SetBounds
title: Add confidence bounds to bounded regions in the k-d tree — SetBounds
text: Recursively set confidence bounds bounded nodes k-d tree.

title: Add confidence bounds to bounded regions in the k-d tree — SetBounds
code: SetBounds(node, ahat, n)

title: Add confidence bounds to bounded regions in the k-d tree — SetBounds
text: node list storing information region. ahat vector significance level depth, starting depth = 0. Output funtion ConfLevel. n Total number observations.

title: Add confidence bounds to bounded regions in the k-d tree — SetBounds
text: updated k-d tree starting input node lower upper filled .

title: Add confidence bounds to bounded regions in the k-d tree — SetBounds
text: $$ F(R_k)\sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n_k+1,n-n_k), $$ \(R_k\) region, \(n\) total number obs. \(n_k\) number obs. \(R_k\), upper lower confidence bounds average density bounded region \(R_k\) depth \(D\) given $$ \text{lower}(R_k) = \frac{qBeta(\hat{\alpha}_D/2, n_k+1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|}\\ \text{upper}(R_k) = \frac{qBeta(1-\hat{\alpha}_D/2, n_k+1, n-n_k)}{|R_k|} $$ \(\hat{\alpha}_D\) significance levels depth \(D\), \(|R_k|\) volume region. select nodes data approximately uniformly distributed, .e., empirical density falls within confidence intervals children. set lower upper bound region $$ \widetilde{\text{lower}} = \max\left(\text{lower}, \text{lower}(\text{1st child}), \text{lower}(\text{2nd child})\right) \\ \widetilde{\text{upper}} = \min\left(\text{upper}, \text{upper}(\text{1st child}), \text{upper}(\text{2nd child})\right), $$ select nodes, pick largest nodes empirical density \(\widetilde{\text{lower}}\)  \(\widetilde{\text{upper}}\). significance levels computed function ConfLevel.

what: Compute the adjacency matrix of regions in the histogram — compute_adjacency_mat

text: Computes adjacency matrix regions Beta tree histogram

title: Compute the adjacency matrix of regions in the histogram — compute_adjacency_mat
code: compute_adjacency_mat(hist, d)

title: Compute the adjacency matrix of regions in the histogram — compute_adjacency_mat
text: hist matrix. row represents one region histogram. histogram constructed d dimensional data, first d columns contain lower bounds coordinate, next d columns contain upper bounds. 2d + 1 column contains empirical density. hist can output BuildHist SelectNodes function. d numeric value data dimension.

title: Compute the adjacency matrix of regions in the histogram — compute_adjacency_mat
text: matrix size N*N, N number regions histogram, .e. number rows hist. \(A_{,j} = 1\) -th j-th regions adjacent 0 otherwise.

title: Compute the adjacency matrix of regions in the histogram — compute_adjacency_mat
text: Two regions neighbors closures intervals direction intersect. example, let two regions \(X\) defined \((X_{,\mathrm{low}}, X_{,\mathrm{}})\) \(Y\) defined \((Y_{,\mathrm{low}}, Y_{,\mathrm{}})\) (\(=1,\ldots, d\)), \(X_{,\mathrm{low}}\) \(X_{,\mathrm{}}\) lower upper bounds dimension \(\) region \(X\). , \(X\) \(Y\) neighbors \([X_{,\mathrm{low}}, X_{,\mathrm{}}] \cap [Y_{,\mathrm{low}}, Y_{,\mathrm{}}] \neq \emptyset\) every \(=1,\ldots, d\).

title: Compute the adjacency matrix of regions in the histogram — compute_adjacency_mat
code: X <-  matrix(rnorm(2000, 0, 1), ncol = 2) B <- BuildHist(X) adj <- compute_adjacency_mat(hist = B, d = 2)
